title: Cohomologically Tropical Varieties
speaker: Kris Shaw
abstract:
This talk asks which tropicalisations of subvarieties of the torus know the cohomology of the original variety. A motivating example are linear embeddings of complements of hyperplane arrangements. 
We can prove that the tropicalisation knows the cohomology of the variety in a strong sense if and only if it satisfies local tropical Poincaré duality and the original variety is so-called “wunderschön”. Following the work of Itenberg, Katzarkov, Mikhalkin, and Zharkov, we can obtain information about the mixed Hodge structure of a family of varieties from its tropicalisation when it locally satisfies the two conditions above.

proof_stages.label: Cohomology-recovery question and basic tropicalization
proof_stages.summary: The talk asks when the cohomology of a complex algebraic variety in a torus can be recovered from its tropicalization, defines the constant-coefficient tropicalization as a logarithmic limit set, and explains that ordinary tropical data preserve some invariants but not cohomology in general.

proof_stages.label: Multitangent cosheaves and tropical cohomology candidates
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces the Itenberg-Katzarkov-Mikhalkin-Zharkov multitangent spaces attached to cones/cells, the functorial maps between them, and the resulting cellular sheaf-cohomology groups H^{p,q} as the tropical vector spaces that could match algebraic cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Linear-space and matroid benchmark
proof_stages.summary: For linear subspaces of a torus, the talk recalls the known chain of results saying that the arrangement matroid determines the Orlik-Solomon cohomology ring, determines the tropicalization, and has that ring recovered from the tropical fan.

proof_stages.label: Tropical compactifications and strata
proof_stages.summary: To formulate a stronger recoverability property, the speaker fixes a unimodular fan supported on the tropicalization, takes the corresponding tropical compactification inside a smooth toric variety, and tracks the open strata and their own tropicalizations.

proof_stages.label: Comparison morphism and cohomologically tropical definition
proof_stages.summary: A comparison map from tropical cohomology to algebraic cohomology is defined by projecting to the diagonal, identifying it with toric Chow data, applying the cycle class map, and restricting to the compactified variety, and a variety is called cohomologically tropical when the resulting maps are isomorphisms on every stratum.

proof_stages.label: Fan-independent if-and-only-if theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that, for a schön subvariety of a torus, being cohomologically tropical is equivalent to the tropicalization being a tropical homology manifold and the variety being wunderschön, and this property is independent of the chosen unimodular fan structure.

proof_stages.label: Tropical homology-manifold and wunderschön criteria
proof_stages.summary: The two conditions in the theorem are unpacked: tropical homology manifolds satisfy local tropical Poincaré duality, while wunderschön varieties have connected strata with pure mixed Hodge structures, yielding strong consequences such as weight-one tropicalization and algebraic divisorial cohomology of stratum closures.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-sequence proof mechanism and applications
proof_stages.summary: The proof is sketched by comparing the Deligne weight spectral sequence for algebraic cohomology with an exact tropical sequence supplied by tropical Poincaré duality, then applications and known sources of examples are described, including recovery of Hodge numbers in degenerating families whose components are cohomologically tropical.

examples.name: Tropical line and three-punctured sphere
examples.note: The line in the two-dimensional torus tropicalizes to the standard three-ray tropical line, illustrating the logarithmic-limit construction, the tropical compactification by three boundary points, and a direct computation of tropical cohomology matching the compactified curve.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the tropicalization of a line through absolute values, amoeba, and skeleton, then later computes the tropical cochain groups and cohomology for the compactified three-punctured sphere example

examples.name: Two curves with the same cross-shaped tropicalization
examples.note: A smooth four-punctured curve and a singular nodal curve have the same tropical cross but different cohomology, forcing the move from naive recovery to extra tropical and Hodge-theoretic hypotheses.

examples.name: Linear subspaces, hyperplane arrangements, and matroid fans
examples.note: Linear subspaces of a torus provide the prototype where the arrangement matroid, Orlik-Solomon algebra, and Bergman fan already recover cohomology, motivating the general cohomologically tropical question.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the linear-subspace case by forming the hyperplane arrangement and matroid, recalling the rank function and Orlik–Solomon algebra, and explaining how the fan recovers cohomology
